topics.title: Approach to the Patient with Cardiovascular Disease
topics.content:
The evaluation of a patient with known or suspected cardiovascular disease (CVD) begins with a thorough history and physical examination. Key symptoms include chest pain/discomfort, dyspnea, palpitations, syncope, fatigue, and edema. The history should characterize the nature, onset, duration, provocative and relieving factors, and associated symptoms.

The New York Heart Association (NYHA) functional classification is used to grade symptom severity:

  • Class I: No limitation of physical activity; ordinary activity does not cause symptoms
  • Class II: Slight limitation of activity; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms
  • Class III: Marked limitation of activity; comfortable at rest; less-than-ordinary activity causes symptoms
  • Class IV: Unable to carry out any physical activity without discomfort; symptoms at rest

topics.title: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System
topics.content:
Vascular Biology

The vascular endothelium is a dynamic organ that regulates vascular tone, inflammation, thrombosis, and vascular remodeling. The endothelium produces nitric oxide (NO), a potent vasodilator synthesized by endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS). Endothelial dysfunction — characterized by reduced NO bioavailability — is an early event in atherosclerosis.

Key endothelial-derived substances:

  • Nitric oxide (NO): Vasodilation, inhibits platelet aggregation and smooth muscle proliferation
  • Prostacyclin (PGI2): Vasodilation and inhibits platelet aggregation
  • Endothelin-1 (ET-1): Potent vasoconstrictor
  • Von Willebrand factor (vWF): Promotes platelet adhesion
Cardiac Contraction and Relaxation

Cardiac myocytes contract through excitation-contraction coupling. The action potential triggers calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (calcium-induced calcium release), which binds troponin C, allowing actin-myosin crossbridge cycling.

Key determinants of cardiac performance:
  • Preload: The ventricular wall stress at end-diastole, determined by ventricular volume. Governed by the Frank-Starling mechanism — increased preload increases stroke volume up to a point.
  • Afterload: The resistance that the ventricle must overcome to eject blood. Determined primarily by systemic vascular resistance and aortic impedance.
  • Contractility (Inotropy): The intrinsic force of contraction independent of preload and afterload. Enhanced by sympathetic stimulation (catecholamines acting on β1-receptors) and digitalis.
  • Heart rate (Chronotropy): Cardiac output = Heart rate × Stroke volume

Cardiac metabolism: The heart is primarily an aerobic organ, deriving ~60-70% of its energy from fatty acid oxidation, with the remainder from glucose, lactate, and ketone bodies. It consumes ~8 mL O2/min per 100g of tissue — the highest of any organ.

topics.title: Epidemiology of Cardiovascular Disease
topics.content:
CVD is the leading cause of death globally, accounting for an estimated 17.9 million deaths annually. The epidemiologic transition describes how CVD patterns change as nations develop economically:

Stages of the Epidemiologic Transition:

  1. Age of pestilence and famine: CVD accounts for <10% of deaths; primarily rheumatic heart disease and infectious cardiomyopathies
  2. Age of receding pandemics: CVD accounts for 10-35% of deaths; rise in hypertensive and rheumatic heart disease
  3. Age of degenerative and man-made diseases: CVD accounts for 35-65% of deaths; dominance of atherosclerotic CVD and stroke
  4. Age of delayed degenerative diseases: CVD accounts for <50% of deaths; decline due to prevention and treatment
  5. Age of inactivity and obesity: Re-emergence of CVD risk due to sedentary lifestyle, obesity, and diabetes

Major modifiable risk factors for atherosclerotic CVD:

  • Hypertension
  • Dyslipidemia (elevated LDL, low HDL)
  • Cigarette smoking
  • Diabetes mellitus
  • Obesity and physical inactivity

Non-modifiable risk factors:

  • Age (men ≥45 years, women ≥55 years)
  • Male sex
  • Family history of premature CVD (first-degree male relative <55 years, female relative <65 years)

topics.title: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
topics.content:
Arterial Pulse

The carotid pulse is the most accurate reflection of the aortic pulse. Key abnormalities:

  • Pulsus parvus et tardus: Small, slowly rising pulse — characteristic of severe aortic stenosis
  • Pulsus bisferiens: Two peaks in systole — seen in combined aortic stenosis/regurgitation or HOCM
  • Pulsus alternans: Alternating strong and weak beats — indicates severe left ventricular dysfunction
  • Pulsus paradoxus: Exaggerated decrease in systolic BP (>10 mmHg) during inspiration — seen in cardiac tamponade, constrictive pericarditis, severe asthma, and COPD
Jugular Venous Pressure (JVP)

The internal jugular vein reflects right atrial pressure. Normal JVP is ≤8 cm H2O (measured as height of venous column above the right atrium, which is approximately 5 cm below the sternal angle).

JVP waveform components:

  • a wave: Atrial contraction (absent in atrial fibrillation)
  • c wave: Tricuspid valve closure/bulging into right atrium during isovolumetric contraction
  • x descent: Atrial relaxation and downward movement of the tricuspid annulus during systole
  • v wave: Passive venous filling of the right atrium while tricuspid valve is closed
  • y descent: Opening of the tricuspid valve and rapid emptying of right atrium into right ventricle
Key JVP abnormalities:
  • Giant a waves (cannon a waves): Atrial contraction against a closed tricuspid valve — complete heart block, ventricular tachycardia, junctional rhythm
  • Large v waves: Tricuspid regurgitation
  • Prominent x descent: Cardiac tamponade, constrictive pericarditis
  • Steep y descent: Constrictive pericarditis, severe tricuspid regurgitation
  • Absent y descent: Cardiac tamponade
  • Kussmaul sign: Paradoxical rise of JVP during inspiration — constrictive pericarditis, restrictive cardiomyopathy, right ventricular infarction, massive PE
Heart Sounds

S1 (First heart sound): Closure of mitral and tricuspid valves at onset of systole

  • Loud S1: Mitral stenosis (mobile valve), short PR interval, high-output states
  • Soft S1: Mitral regurgitation, long PR interval, low-output states, calcified immobile mitral valve

S2 (Second heart sound): Closure of aortic (A2) and pulmonic (P2) valves

  • Normal physiologic splitting: Widens with inspiration (delayed P2 due to increased venous return to RV)
  • Wide fixed splitting: Atrial septal defect (ASD)
  • Paradoxical splitting: Reversal of normal pattern — widening with expiration. Causes: LBBB, severe aortic stenosis, RV pacing, right ventricular pre-excitation (WPW), systemic hypertension
  • Wide splitting: RBBB, pulmonary stenosis, pulmonary hypertension

S3 (Third heart sound): Low-pitched sound in early diastole, during rapid ventricular filling

  • In young adults: May be normal (physiologic)
  • In adults >40 years: Pathologic — indicates ventricular volume overload and elevated filling pressures. Associated with heart failure, mitral regurgitation, tricuspid regurgitation, VSD

S4 (Fourth heart sound): Low-pitched sound in late diastole, produced by atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle

  • Always pathologic
  • Associated with: ventricular hypertrophy (hypertension, aortic stenosis, HOCM), ischemic heart disease, acute MI
  • Never heard in atrial fibrillation (no organized atrial contraction)
Cardiac Murmurs

Systolic murmurs:

  • Ejection (crescendo-decrescendo): Aortic stenosis, pulmonic stenosis, HOCM, flow murmurs
  • Pansystolic (holosystolic): Mitral regurgitation, tricuspid regurgitation, VSD

Diastolic murmurs:

  • Early diastolic (decrescendo): Aortic regurgitation, pulmonic regurgitation (Graham Steell murmur in pulmonary hypertension)
  • Mid-diastolic (rumbling): Mitral stenosis (with presystolic accentuation if in sinus rhythm), tricuspid stenosis, Austin Flint murmur (severe AR)

Continuous murmurs: Patent ductus arteriosus (machinery murmur), arteriovenous fistula, coronary AV fistula

Dynamic auscultation maneuvers:

  • Valsalva strain phase / Standing: Decreases preload → Most murmurs decrease; BUT HOCM and MVP murmurs increase (HOCM murmur becomes louder and longer; MVP click moves earlier)
  • Squatting / Passive leg elevation: Increases preload → Most murmurs increase; HOCM and MVP murmurs decrease
  • Inspiration: Increases venous return to right heart → Right-sided murmurs increase (Carvallo sign: TR murmur increases with inspiration)

topics.title: Electrophysiologic Background
topics.content:
The ECG records the electrical activity of the heart from the body surface. The standard 12-lead ECG consists of 6 limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF) and 6 precordial leads (V1-V6).

Normal ECG intervals:

  • PR interval: 0.12-0.20 seconds (120-200 ms) — represents atrial depolarization and AV conduction delay
  • QRS duration: <0.12 seconds (<120 ms) — represents ventricular depolarization
  • QT interval: Rate-dependent; corrected QT (QTc) using Bazett's formula: QTc = QT/√RR. Normal QTc: <440 ms (men), <460 ms (women)

Heart rate calculation: 300 / number of large boxes between R-R intervals; or count R waves in 10 seconds × 6

topics.title: Normal ECG Waveforms
topics.content:
P wave: Represents atrial depolarization. Normal duration <120 ms, amplitude <2.5 mm in lead II. Best seen in leads II and V1.

  • P mitrale (bifid P wave in lead II, with interpeak duration ≥40 ms): Left atrial enlargement
  • P pulmonale (peaked P wave >2.5 mm in lead II): Right atrial enlargement

QRS complex: Represents ventricular depolarization. Normal septal depolarization proceeds left to right, producing small q waves in leads I, aVL, V5, V6 and a small r wave in V1.

T wave: Represents ventricular repolarization. Normally upright in leads I, II, V3-V6; inverted in aVR; variable in III, aVL, V1-V2.

U wave: Small deflection after the T wave. Prominent U waves may be seen with hypokalemia, bradycardia, or LV hypertrophy.

topics.title: Cardiac Axis
topics.content:
Normal QRS axis: −30° to +90°

  • Left axis deviation (LAD): More negative than −30°. Causes: left anterior fascicular block (LAFB), inferior MI, LVH, WPW (right-sided accessory pathway), COPD with RVH
  • Right axis deviation (RAD): More positive than +90°. Causes: right ventricular hypertrophy, left posterior fascicular block (LPFB), lateral MI, PE, COPD, normal in children/young adults
  • Extreme/Northwest axis: −90° to ±180°. Causes: ventricular tachycardia, severe RVH, hyperkalemia

topics.title: Ventricular Hypertrophy
topics.content:
Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) — Voltage criteria:

  • Sokolow-Lyon: S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 ≥35 mm
  • Cornell: R in aVL + S in V3 >28 mm (men), >20 mm (women)
  • R in aVL >11 mm

Associated features: Left atrial enlargement, ST depression and T wave inversion in lateral leads ("strain pattern"), left axis deviation.

Right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH):

  • R > S in V1 (R/S ratio >1), right axis deviation
  • Associated features: Right atrial enlargement, ST-T changes in right precordial leads, deep S waves in V5-V6

topics.title: Bundle Branch Blocks
topics.content:
Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB) — QRS ≥120 ms:

  • rsR' pattern in V1-V2 (M-shaped)
  • Wide, slurred S wave in leads I, V5-V6
  • Causes: Normal variant, ASD, PE, RV overload, IHD, myocarditis

Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB) — QRS ≥120 ms:

  • Broad, notched R wave in leads I, aVL, V5-V6
  • Absent septal q waves in leads I, V5-V6
  • QS or rS in V1
  • Secondary ST-T changes (discordant with QRS direction)
  • Causes: IHD, hypertension, cardiomyopathy, aortic valve disease
  • LBBB always indicates underlying heart disease and precludes standard MI interpretation on ECG

Left Anterior Fascicular Block (LAFB):

  • Left axis deviation (more negative than −45°)
  • qR pattern in leads I, aVL
  • rS pattern in leads II, III, aVF
  • QRS duration slightly prolonged but <120 ms

Left Posterior Fascicular Block (LPFB):

  • Right axis deviation (>+90°)
  • rS pattern in leads I, aVL
  • qR pattern in leads II, III, aVF
  • Must exclude other causes of RAD (RVH, lateral MI)
  • Less common than LAFB (left posterior fascicle has dual blood supply)

Bifascicular block: RBBB + LAFB (most common) or RBBB + LPFB

Trifascicular block: Bifascicular block + first-degree AV block (suggests extensive conduction system disease)

topics.title: Myocardial Ischemia and Infarction on ECG
topics.content:
Acute STEMI evolution:

  1. Hyperacute T waves: Tall, peaked, symmetrical T waves — earliest change (minutes)
  2. ST elevation: Convex upward ST elevation in contiguous leads (minutes to hours)
  3. Pathologic Q waves: ≥1 mm wide (40 ms) and ≥25% of R wave height — indicate transmural necrosis (hours to days)
  4. T wave inversion: As ST segment normalizes
  5. Persistent Q waves: May remain permanently; ST and T changes resolve over weeks

Localization of MI by ECG leads:

  • Anterior: V1-V4 (LAD territory)
  • Lateral: I, aVL, V5-V6 (LCx territory)
  • Inferior: II, III, aVF (RCA territory, less commonly LCx)
  • Posterior: Reciprocal changes in V1-V3 (tall R wave, ST depression, upright T) — equivalent to ST elevation in posterior leads V7-V9. Usually RCA or LCx territory
  • Right ventricular: ST elevation in right-sided leads (V3R-V6R), particularly V4R (most sensitive for RV infarction)

NSTEMI/Unstable angina ECG patterns:

  • ST depression (horizontal or downsloping is more specific than upsloping)
  • T wave inversion (deep symmetrical T wave inversions suggest critical proximal LAD stenosis — Wellens syndrome)
  • May have normal ECG

topics.title: Metabolic and Drug Effects on ECG
topics.content:
Hyperkalemia (progressive changes with rising K+):

  1. Peaked, narrow-based T waves (earliest sign)
  2. PR prolongation, flattened P waves
  3. QRS widening
  4. Sine-wave pattern → Ventricular fibrillation / asystole

Hypokalemia:

  • ST depression, T wave flattening/inversion
  • Prominent U waves
  • Prolonged QT/QU interval

Hypercalcemia: Short QT interval (shortened ST segment) Hypocalcemia: Prolonged QT interval (prolonged ST segment)

Digitalis effect: "Salvador Dali mustache" — downsloping ST depression with scooped appearance; shortened QT; T wave flattening/inversion. This represents drug effect, not toxicity.

Digitalis toxicity: Any arrhythmia, but characteristically atrial tachycardia with AV block, bidirectional VT, accelerated junctional rhythm

Electrical alternans: Beat-to-beat alternation in QRS amplitude — classic for large pericardial effusion / cardiac tamponade

topics.title: Echocardiography
topics.content:
Echocardiography is the primary imaging method for assessing cardiac structure and function. It is widely available, portable, non-invasive, and involves no ionizing radiation.

Key applications:
  • Assessment of LV and RV systolic and diastolic function
  • Evaluation of valvular heart disease (severity of stenosis and regurgitation)
  • Pericardial disease (effusion, tamponade, constriction)
  • Cardiac masses (thrombus, tumor, vegetation)
  • Congenital heart disease
  • Hemodynamic assessment using Doppler

Left ventricular systolic function:

  • Ejection fraction (EF) is the primary measure: normal LVEF ≥55%
  • LVEF 40-54%: Mildly reduced
  • LVEF <40%: Reduced (HFrEF)
  • Newer methods: Speckle-tracking strain imaging for early detection of systolic dysfunction

Left ventricular diastolic function (assessed by Doppler):

  • E/A ratio (mitral inflow): E = early filling, A = atrial contraction
  • e' (tissue Doppler): Mitral annular relaxation velocity
  • E/e' ratio: Correlates with LV filling pressures (E/e' >14 suggests elevated filling pressure)
  • Grades of diastolic dysfunction:
    • Grade I (impaired relaxation): E/A <0.8, normal filling pressures
    • Grade II (pseudonormal): E/A 0.8-1.5, elevated filling pressures (distinguished from normal by e' and response to Valsalva)
    • Grade III (restrictive): E/A ≥2, markedly elevated filling pressures

Right ventricular function:

  • CMR is the most accurate noninvasive technique for RV assessment
  • Echocardiographic parameters: TAPSE (tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion), fractional area change (FAC)
  • McConnell sign: Preserved apical RV wall motion with basal/mid free wall akinesis — highly specific for acute PE

Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE): Superior for posterior structures (left atrium, mitral valve, interatrial septum, aorta). Indications include infective endocarditis (especially prosthetic valves), aortic dissection, intracardiac thrombus (especially left atrial appendage), and intraoperative guidance.

topics.title: Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR)
topics.content:
CMR provides high-resolution imaging of cardiac structure and function without ionizing radiation. Key pulse sequences:

  • Cine imaging: LV/RV volume and function (gold standard for volumes and EF)
  • Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE): Myocardial infarction and fibrosis. Detected acute and chronic infarcts with sensitivity of 99% and 94%, respectively.
    • Subendocardial/transmural LGE in a coronary distribution → Ischemic etiology
    • Mid-wall or epicardial LGE → Nonischemic cardiomyopathy (myocarditis, sarcoidosis, DCM)
  • T1 and T2 mapping: T1 for fibrosis, T2 for myocardial edema (active inflammation)
  • Perfusion imaging: Myocardial perfusion with pharmacologic stress (vasodilator agent or dobutamine)
  • Iron content imaging (T2*): Myocardial iron infiltration (hemochromatosis, thalassemia)

CMR indications:

  • Assessment of cardiomyopathy etiology (ischemic vs nonischemic)
  • Myocarditis (LGE in mid-wall/epicardial pattern, sparing subendocardium)
  • Cardiac amyloidosis (diffuse subendocardial LGE, inability to null myocardium)
  • Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (fibrosis detection, risk stratification)
  • Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
  • Cardiac sarcoidosis
  • Iron overload quantification

Safety: MRI-conditional devices (pacemakers, ICDs) can be safely scanned at 1.5T under appropriate protocols. Gadolinium-based contrast agents (GBCAs) — use macrocyclic agents; avoid in severe renal dysfunction (eGFR <30) due to risk of nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) with older linear agents.

topics.title: Cardiac CT
topics.content:
Coronary CT angiography (CTA):

  • Sensitivity 99%, specificity 89% for detecting >50% coronary stenosis
  • Excellent negative predictive value — effectively excludes obstructive CAD
  • Most useful in low-to-intermediate risk patients with acute chest pain
  • Limitations: Blooming artifact from heavy calcification, metallic stents

Coronary artery calcium (CAC) score (Agatston score):

  • Quantifies coronary atherosclerotic burden
  • CAC = 0: Very low risk of obstructive CAD
  • CAC >400: High likelihood of obstructive CAD
  • Note: CAC scores are not specific markers of obstructive stenosis

Radiation exposure: Current average coronary CTA dose: 3-15 mSv (can be as low as 1 mSv with newer techniques). Compare: invasive coronary angiography ~7 mSv; SPECT perfusion ~4-11 mSv; PET perfusion ~2-4 mSv.

topics.title: Nuclear Cardiology (Stress Imaging)
topics.content:
SPECT myocardial perfusion imaging:

  • Most common stress imaging test for CAD evaluation
  • Reversible perfusion defect = Ischemia
  • Fixed perfusion defect = Prior infarction/scar
  • Normal study: <1% annual rate of major adverse cardiac events
  • Sensitivity 88%, specificity 76% for detecting CAD

PET myocardial perfusion imaging:

  • Higher sensitivity (93%) and spatial resolution than SPECT
  • Provides quantitative myocardial blood flow and coronary flow reserve
  • Particularly useful for detecting balanced ischemia in multivessel CAD

Comparative diagnostic accuracy for CAD:

ModalitySensitivitySpecificity
Exercise ECG (treadmill)68%77%
Exercise echocardiography84%82%
Dobutamine echocardiography80%84%
SPECT MPI88%76%
PET perfusion93%81%
CMR perfusion91%81%
Coronary CTA99%89%

Stress testing selection:

  • Can exercise + interpretable resting ECG: Exercise treadmill test (ETT) first
  • Cannot exercise: Pharmacologic stress (vasodilator: adenosine/regadenoson for perfusion imaging; dobutamine for echo/CMR)
  • Abnormal resting ECG (LBBB, LVH with strain, digoxin, paced rhythm): Imaging-based stress test (perfusion or echo), not exercise ECG alone

topics.title: Principles of Clinical Cardiac Electrophysiology
topics.content:
Cardiac Action Potential

Phases of the ventricular action potential:

  • Phase 0 (Rapid depolarization): Fast Na+ channel opening → rapid upstroke
  • Phase 1 (Early repolarization): Transient outward K+ current (Ito)
  • Phase 2 (Plateau): Balance of inward Ca2+ current (L-type channels) and outward K+ currents
  • Phase 3 (Repolarization): Outward K+ currents (IKr, IKs) predominate
  • Phase 4 (Resting potential): Maintained by IK1 (inward rectifier K+ channel) at ~−90 mV in ventricular myocytes

Pacemaker cells (SA node, AV node):

  • Phase 0: Slow upstroke mediated by L-type Ca2+ channels (not fast Na+ channels)
  • Phase 4: Spontaneous diastolic depolarization (automaticity) driven by funny current (If) and T-type Ca2+ channels
  • This explains why calcium channel blockers and beta blockers affect SA/AV node function
Mechanisms of Arrhythmias
  1. Enhanced automaticity: Increased rate of spontaneous depolarization in latent pacemaker cells (e.g., accelerated junctional rhythm, some atrial tachycardias). Catecholamines, hypoxia, hypokalemia, and ischemia can enhance automaticity.
  2. Triggered activity: Afterdepolarizations — oscillations in membrane potential that reach threshold and trigger a new action potential
    • Early afterdepolarizations (EADs): Occur during phase 2 or 3, in setting of prolonged repolarization (long QT). Mechanism of Torsades de Pointes.
    • Delayed afterdepolarizations (DADs): Occur after full repolarization (phase 4), caused by intracellular calcium overload. Mechanism of digitalis-toxic arrhythmias and catecholaminergic polymorphic VT (CPVT).
  3. Reentry: The most common mechanism of sustained arrhythmias. Requirements:
    • Two functionally distinct pathways
    • Unidirectional block in one pathway
    • Slow conduction in the other pathway
    • Re-excitation of the initially blocked pathway after its refractory period
    • Examples: AVNRT, AVRT, atrial flutter, scar-related VT
Vaughan Williams Classification of Antiarrhythmic Drugs
  • Class I (Na+ channel blockers):
    • Ia (moderate Na+ block, prolong repolarization): Quinidine, procainamide, disopyramide. Prolong QT, risk of Torsades.
    • Ib (weak Na+ block, shorten repolarization): Lidocaine, mexiletine. Primarily for ventricular arrhythmias.
    • Ic (strong Na+ block, minimal effect on repolarization): Flecainide, propafenone. Contraindicated in structural heart disease (CAST trial — increased mortality in post-MI patients).
  • Class II (Beta-adrenergic blockers): Metoprolol, atenolol, propranolol, esmolol. Decrease automaticity and AV conduction. First-line for many supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias.
  • Class III (K+ channel blockers — prolong repolarization): Amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide, ibutilide. Prolong QT interval. Amiodarone is the most effective antiarrhythmic but has significant extracardiac toxicities (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic, ocular, cutaneous, neurologic).
  • Class IV (Ca2+ channel blockers): Verapamil, diltiazem. Slow AV conduction. Used for rate control in AF/AFL and termination of AVNRT/AVRT.

topics.title: Bradyarrhythmias — Sinus Node Disease
topics.content:
Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND) / Sick Sinus Syndrome:

The sinus node is the dominant cardiac pacemaker, located at the junction of the superior vena cava and right atrium. Its intrinsic rate is 60-100 bpm, modulated by autonomic tone.

Manifestations of SND:

  • Sinus bradycardia (inappropriate for physiologic state)
  • Sinus pauses / sinus arrest
  • Sinoatrial exit block
  • Chronotropic incompetence (failure to achieve ≥80% of age-predicted maximal heart rate during exercise)
  • Tachy-brady syndrome: Alternating tachyarrhythmias (typically AF) and bradycardia

Causes:

  • Idiopathic degenerative fibrosis (most common — age-related)
  • Drugs: Beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, amiodarone, lithium
  • Ischemia (RCA supplies SA node in ~60% of patients)
  • Infiltrative diseases (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis)
  • Inflammatory conditions (myocarditis, pericarditis)
  • Post-cardiac surgery

Treatment:

  • Permanent pacemaker for symptomatic bradycardia
  • Preferred mode: AAI or DDD (with minimal ventricular pacing) rather than VVI to reduce risk of pacemaker syndrome and AF

topics.title: Bradyarrhythmias — AV Conduction Disease
topics.content:
First-degree AV block: PR interval >200 ms. All P waves conducted. Generally benign. No treatment required.

Second-degree AV block:

  • Type I (Mobitz I / Wenckebach): Progressive PR prolongation until a dropped QRS. Block is usually at the AV node. Usually benign; may be seen in trained athletes, during sleep, or with drugs.
  • Type II (Mobitz II): Constant PR interval with sudden dropped QRS. Block is usually infranodal (His-Purkinje). More likely to progress to complete heart block. Pacemaker indicated.

Third-degree (Complete) AV block: Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS complexes. Escape rhythm:

  • If AV nodal block: Junctional escape (40-60 bpm, narrow QRS, relatively stable)
  • If infranodal block: Ventricular escape (20-40 bpm, wide QRS, unreliable)

Indications for permanent pacemaker in AV block:

  • Third-degree AV block (regardless of symptoms)
  • Symptomatic second-degree AV block
  • Mobitz type II second-degree AV block (even if asymptomatic)

Lev's disease: Idiopathic fibrocalcific degeneration of the conduction system associated with calcification of the mitral and aortic annuli and the central fibrous body. Most common cause of chronic AV block in elderly.

Lenègre's disease: Idiopathic sclerodegenerative disease involving the His bundle and bundle branches.

topics.title: Supraventricular Tachyarrhythmias
topics.content:
Sinus Tachycardia

Normal sinus tachycardia is a physiologic response to stimuli (exercise, fever, pain, anxiety, hypovolemia, anemia, thyrotoxicosis, heart failure). Treatment addresses the underlying cause.

Inappropriate sinus tachycardia (IST): Resting heart rate >100 bpm or exaggerated rate response to minimal activity without identifiable cause. Treatment: ivabradine (If channel blocker) or beta blockers.

Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS): Heart rate increase ≥30 bpm (or absolute rate >120 bpm) within 10 minutes of standing, without orthostatic hypotension. More common in young women.

Focal Atrial Tachycardia

P wave morphology different from sinus P waves; atrial rate typically 150-250 bpm. May be automatic, triggered, or micro-reentrant. Treatment: beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, catheter ablation. Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT): ≥3 different P wave morphologies with irregular rhythm — commonly associated with COPD and acute illness; treatment is directed at underlying condition; verapamil may be useful.

Paroxysmal SVTs

AV Nodal Reentrant Tachycardia (AVNRT):

  • Most common paroxysmal SVT
  • Reentrant circuit within or near the AV node using fast and slow pathways
  • Typical AVNRT: Slow-fast (antegrade conduction via slow pathway, retrograde via fast pathway) — RP interval very short; P waves buried in or just after QRS (pseudo-r' in V1, pseudo-S in inferior leads)
  • Atypical AVNRT: Fast-slow — long RP tachycardia with inverted P waves before QRS
  • Acute termination: Vagal maneuvers → IV adenosine → IV verapamil/diltiazem → IV beta blockers
  • Prevention: Catheter ablation of slow pathway (cure rate >95%)

AV Reentrant Tachycardia (AVRT):

  • Uses an accessory pathway (bypass tract) connecting atrium and ventricle
  • Orthodromic AVRT (most common): Antegrade conduction via AV node, retrograde via accessory pathway → Narrow complex tachycardia with P waves after QRS
  • Antidromic AVRT: Antegrade via accessory pathway, retrograde via AV node → Wide complex tachycardia (pre-excited QRS)

Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome:

  • Pre-excitation pattern on ECG: Short PR interval (<120 ms), delta wave, widened QRS
  • Risk of sudden death if atrial fibrillation develops: Rapid antegrade conduction via accessory pathway → Ventricular fibrillation
  • AVOID AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, verapamil, digoxin) in pre-excited AF — these may accelerate conduction via the accessory pathway
  • Treatment of pre-excited AF: IV procainamide or ibutilide; or DC cardioversion if hemodynamically unstable
  • Definitive treatment: Catheter ablation of accessory pathway
Atrial Flutter (Chapter 250)
  • Typical (CTI-dependent) atrial flutter: Macro-reentrant circuit in the right atrium using the cavotricuspid isthmus (CTI)
  • Atrial rate ~300 bpm with "sawtooth" flutter waves best seen in leads II, III, aVF
  • Ventricular rate typically ~150 bpm (2:1 conduction) — any regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm should raise suspicion for atrial flutter with 2:1 block
  • Treatment: Rate control with AV nodal blockers; rhythm control with catheter ablation of CTI (cure rate >90%); anticoagulation same as for AF
  • Cardioversion: Lower energy often effective (50 J); ibutilide for pharmacologic cardioversion
Atrial Fibrillation (Chapter 251)

The most common sustained arrhythmia, affecting ~33.5 million people worldwide. Prevalence increases with age (8-10% in those >80 years).

Classification:

  • Paroxysmal: Self-terminating within 7 days
  • Persistent: Sustained >7 days, requiring intervention for termination
  • Long-standing persistent: Continuous AF >12 months
  • Permanent: AF accepted by patient and physician; no further rhythm control attempted

Risk factors and associated conditions: Hypertension (most common), heart failure, valvular disease (especially mitral stenosis), obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, thyrotoxicosis, diabetes, alcohol excess ("holiday heart"), pulmonary disease, cardiac surgery, advancing age.

Thromboembolic Risk Assessment — CHA2DS2-VASc Score:

Risk FactorPoints
Congestive heart failure1
Hypertension1
Age ≥75 years2
Diabetes mellitus1
Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism2
Vascular disease (MI, PAD, aortic plaque)1
Age 65-74 years1
Sex category (female)1
  • Score 0 (males) or 1 (females): No anticoagulation needed
  • Score 1 (males): Consider anticoagulation
  • Score ≥2: Oral anticoagulation recommended (DOACs preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF)

Bleeding Risk — HAS-BLED Score: Hypertension, Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke, Bleeding history, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. Score ≥3 = high bleeding risk — does NOT contraindicate anticoagulation but should prompt attention to modifiable risk factors.

Rate Control:

  • Target resting heart rate <110 bpm (lenient) or <80 bpm (strict) — RACE II trial showed lenient control was non-inferior
  • Drugs: Beta blockers (metoprolol, atenolol), non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil — avoid in HFrEF), digoxin (add-on, not first-line)
  • AV node ablation + permanent pacemaker (pace and ablate) for refractory rate control

Rhythm Control:

  • Cardioversion: Electrical (synchronized DC shock) or pharmacologic (flecainide, propafenone for no structural heart disease; amiodarone for structural heart disease; ibutilide for recent-onset)
  • If AF duration >48 hours or unknown: Either TEE to exclude left atrial thrombus before cardioversion OR 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation before cardioversion. Continue anticoagulation for ≥4 weeks after cardioversion regardless.
  • Antiarrhythmic drugs for maintenance: Flecainide/propafenone (no structural heart disease), sotalol, dronedarone (no HFrEF or permanent AF), amiodarone
  • Catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation): Superior to antiarrhythmic drugs for maintaining sinus rhythm. Recommended for symptomatic AF refractory to or intolerant of antiarrhythmic drugs. Increasingly used as first-line therapy.

topics.title: Ventricular Arrhythmias
topics.content:
Premature Ventricular Complexes (PVCs)

Common and often benign. Wide QRS (>120 ms) with abnormal morphology, not preceded by a P wave, followed by a compensatory pause (usually).

  • Frequent PVCs (>10-15% of all beats) can cause PVC-induced cardiomyopathy (reversible with PVC suppression via ablation or antiarrhythmic drugs)
  • RVOT PVCs (most common idiopathic site): LBBB morphology with inferior axis
  • LVOT PVCs: RBBB morphology with inferior axis
Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)

Nonsustained VT (NSVT): ≥3 consecutive ventricular beats at rate >100 bpm, lasting <30 seconds. Management depends on underlying heart disease:

  • Structurally normal heart: Usually benign; treat if symptomatic
  • Structural heart disease (post-MI, cardiomyopathy): May warrant further risk stratification for sudden cardiac death

Sustained VT: ≥30 seconds or requiring termination due to hemodynamic compromise

Monomorphic VT: Single QRS morphology. Most commonly scar-related reentry (post-MI). QRS morphology predicts origin:

  • LBBB morphology → RV origin
  • RBBB morphology → LV origin

VT vs SVT with aberrancy (wide-complex tachycardia differential): Favoring VT:

  • AV dissociation (most reliable sign)
  • Capture beats and fusion beats
  • Very wide QRS (>140 ms with RBBB pattern, >160 ms with LBBB pattern)
  • Concordant precordial QRS pattern (all positive or all negative)
  • Northwest axis
  • History of structural heart disease
  • Brugada algorithm and Vereckei algorithm help differentiate
  • When in doubt, treat as VT — it is the more dangerous diagnosis

Treatment of sustained VT:

  • Hemodynamically unstable: Immediate synchronized cardioversion (or defibrillation if pulseless)
  • Hemodynamically stable: IV amiodarone, IV procainamide, or IV lidocaine
  • Long-term: ICD implantation, antiarrhythmic drugs, catheter ablation
Polymorphic VT and Ventricular Fibrillation

Torsades de Pointes (TdP):

  • Polymorphic VT with twisting of the QRS axis around the baseline
  • Occurs in the setting of prolonged QT interval
  • Acquired long QT (more common): Drugs (antiarrhythmics Ia and III, antibiotics — macrolides/fluoroquinolones, antipsychotics, methadone), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypothermia, bradycardia, CNS injury
  • Treatment: IV magnesium (first-line, even with normal Mg levels), overdrive pacing (increase heart rate to shorten QT), isoproterenol, correct electrolytes, discontinue offending drug
  • Avoid all QT-prolonging drugs; avoid Class Ia and III antiarrhythmics

Congenital Long QT Syndrome (LQTS):

TypeGeneChannel AffectedTriggersECG Pattern
LQT1KCNQ1IKs (slow K+)Exercise (especially swimming)Broad-based T waves
LQT2KCNH2 (HERG)IKr (rapid delayed rectifier K+)Auditory stimuli, emotional stressLow-amplitude, notched T waves
LQT3SCN5AINa (Na+ gain of function)Rest/sleepLate-onset T wave, long ST segment

Treatment: Beta blockers (first-line, especially for LQT1 and LQT2); ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or high-risk patients; mexiletine may be useful in LQT3 (Na+ channel blocker).

Brugada Syndrome:

  • Autosomal dominant, mutation in SCN5A (Na+ channel — loss of function) in ~20-25% of cases
  • ECG: Coved-type ST elevation (≥2 mm) with T wave inversion in V1-V3 (Type 1 pattern)
  • Higher prevalence in males and in Southeast Asian populations
  • Risk of VF, especially at rest or during sleep
  • Treatment: ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or syncope with spontaneous type 1 pattern; quinidine may be useful; catheter ablation of epicardial substrate is emerging therapy
  • Avoid drugs that unmask Brugada pattern (www.brugadadrugs.org): flecainide, procainamide, Class Ia/Ic agents, fever

Catecholaminergic Polymorphic VT (CPVT):

  • Mutations in ryanodine receptor (RYR2) or calsequestrin (CASQ2)
  • Exercise or emotional stress triggers bidirectional VT or polymorphic VT
  • Structurally normal heart, normal resting ECG, normal QT
  • Treatment: Beta blockers (nadolol preferred), flecainide as add-on, ICD if refractory, left cardiac sympathetic denervation
Electrical Storm (Chapter 256)

Defined as ≥3 episodes of sustained VT or VF within 24 hours.

Management:

  • IV amiodarone (first-line)
  • Deep sedation
  • Sympathetic blockade: IV beta blockers (esmolol), stellate ganglion block
  • Catheter ablation for refractory cases
  • Mechanical circulatory support if hemodynamically compromised
  • Treat underlying cause (ischemia, electrolyte imbalances)

topics.title: Myocarditis
topics.content:
Inflammation of the myocardium, most commonly caused by viral infection. Can present with acute heart failure, arrhythmias, or sudden death.

Etiologies:

Viral (most common in developed countries):

  • Enteroviruses (coxsackievirus B), adenoviruses, parvovirus B19, HHV6
  • SARS-CoV-2: COVID-19-associated myocarditis; may range from mild/subclinical to fulminant; endomyocardial biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltrates; treatment is supportive; role of immunosuppression unclear
  • HIV

Other infectious:

  • Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi): Most common cause of infectious cardiomyopathy globally. Endemic in Central/South America. Acute phase: Often subclinical. Chronic phase (years later): Dilated cardiomyopathy, conduction abnormalities (RBBB most common), apical aneurysm, thromboembolic events. Treatment: Benznidazole or nifurtimox (in acute phase); cardiac treatment is supportive.
  • Diphtheria: Toxin-mediated myocarditis, can cause conduction abnormalities
  • Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi): AV block (especially high-grade) is the most common cardiac manifestation; usually reversible with antibiotics

Noninfectious:

  • Giant cell myocarditis: Rapidly progressive heart failure, ventricular arrhythmias, high mortality. Biopsy shows multinucleated giant cells. Treatment: Immunosuppression (cyclosporine + corticosteroids); often requires cardiac transplantation.
  • Cardiac sarcoidosis: Noncaseating granulomas. Can cause conduction abnormalities, ventricular arrhythmias, heart failure. Diagnosis: CMR (patchy LGE, especially basal septum), PET (FDG uptake in active disease). Treatment: Corticosteroids, steroid-sparing agents.
  • Eosinophilic myocarditis: May be idiopathic, drug-related (hypersensitivity), or associated with hypereosinophilic syndrome

Diagnosis:

  • Clinical: Chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, signs of heart failure; often preceded by viral illness
  • Elevated troponin, BNP
  • ECG: ST-T changes, arrhythmias
  • CMR: Myocardial edema on T2 imaging, mid-wall/epicardial LGE (distinguishes from MI which shows subendocardial LGE)
  • Endomyocardial biopsy: Dallas criteria (lymphocytic infiltrates with myocyte necrosis); immunohistochemistry improves sensitivity

topics.title: Cardiomyopathies
topics.content:
Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)

Characterized by ventricular dilatation and impaired systolic function. Most common indication for cardiac transplantation.

Etiologies:

  • Idiopathic (~50%)
  • Familial/genetic (~20-35%): TTN mutations (truncating variants in titin gene — most common genetic cause), lamin A/C (LMNA — associated with conduction disease and arrhythmias), MYH7, SCN5A
  • Muscular dystrophies: Duchenne (dystrophin), Emery-Dreifuss (emerin/lamin)
  • Alcoholic cardiomyopathy: Typically >80g/day for >5 years; may be partially reversible with abstinence
  • Chemotherapy: Anthracyclines (doxorubicin — dose-dependent, cumulative toxicity, irreversible), trastuzumab (non-dose-dependent, often reversible), immune checkpoint inhibitors (myocarditis), tyrosine kinase inhibitors, proteasome inhibitors (carfilzomib)
  • Peripartum cardiomyopathy: Last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum; risk factors include multiparity, advanced maternal age, preeclampsia, African descent; may recover or progress; subsequent pregnancies carry risk of recurrence
  • Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy: Reversible with rate/rhythm control
  • Metabolic: Thyroid disease, pheochromocytoma, hemochromatosis, nutritional deficiency (thiamine/beriberi, selenium/Keshan disease)
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)

Autosomal dominant; most common genetic cardiovascular disease (~1:500 prevalence). Characterized by unexplained LV hypertrophy (wall thickness ≥15 mm).

Genetics: Mutations in sarcomeric proteins — most commonly β-myosin heavy chain (MYH7) and myosin-binding protein C (MYBPC3).

Pathophysiology: LVH → diastolic dysfunction; dynamic LVOT obstruction (in ~70% with provocation) due to systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve.

Clinical features: Dyspnea, angina, syncope (especially exertional), palpitations, sudden cardiac death.

Sudden cardiac death risk factors: Family history of SCD, unexplained syncope, massive LVH (≥30 mm), NSVT on Holter, abnormal BP response to exercise, extensive LGE on CMR.

Takotsubo (Stress) Cardiomyopathy

Transient LV systolic dysfunction with characteristic apical ballooning, triggered by emotional or physical stress. Predominantly affects postmenopausal women. Mimics acute MI but coronary arteries are normal. Typically recovers within weeks. Possible mechanism: Catecholamine-mediated myocardial stunning.

Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC)

Fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium. Mutations in desmosomal proteins (plakophilin-2 most common, desmoglein-2, desmocollin-2, plakoglobin, desmoplakin). Clinical: Ventricular arrhythmias arising from the RV (LBBB morphology), RV failure, SCD (especially in young athletes). ECG: Epsilon waves (small positive deflections at end of QRS in V1-V3), T wave inversions in V1-V3. Diagnosis: Revised 2010 Task Force Criteria (imaging, ECG, arrhythmic, histologic, genetic, family history criteria). Exercise restriction is recommended.

topics.title: Cardiac Transplantation
topics.content:
Indications: End-stage heart failure refractory to medical therapy (typically NYHA Class III-IV) with expected 1-year survival <50% without transplantation.

Immunosuppression: Triple therapy — calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus or cyclosporine), antimetabolite (mycophenolate mofetil), and corticosteroids (tapered over time).

Rejection:

  • Acute cellular rejection (ACR): Lymphocytic infiltration of the myocardium; diagnosed by endomyocardial biopsy; treated with pulse corticosteroids
  • Antibody-mediated rejection (AMR): Donor-specific antibodies; diagnosed by C4d staining on biopsy; treatment includes plasmapheresis, IVIG, rituximab

Cardiac allograft vasculopathy (CAV): Diffuse, concentric intimal hyperplasia affecting the entire coronary tree (unlike focal atherosclerosis). Leading cause of late graft failure and death. May not cause typical angina due to denervation. Diagnosed by intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) or angiography.

topics.title: Mechanical Circulatory Support
topics.content:
Left ventricular assist devices (LVADs):

  • Continuous-flow devices (HeartMate 3 — centrifugal flow; HeartMate II — axial flow)
  • HeartMate 3: Lower rate of pump thrombosis than HeartMate II; fully magnetically levitated rotor
  • Used as bridge to transplant, bridge to decision, or destination therapy
  • Complications: Bleeding (GI arteriovenous malformations due to loss of pulsatility and acquired von Willebrand syndrome), pump thrombosis, driveline infection, right heart failure, stroke

topics.title: Aortic Stenosis (AS)
topics.content:
Etiology:

  • Degenerative calcific (age-related): Most common cause in elderly (>70 years)
  • Bicuspid aortic valve: Most common cause in those <70 years; ~1-2% prevalence; associated with aortopathy (ascending aortic dilatation)
  • Rheumatic: Usually combined with mitral valve disease; commissural fusion

Pathophysiology: Progressive obstruction → LV pressure overload → Concentric LV hypertrophy → Eventually decompensation with systolic dysfunction. Reduced coronary flow reserve despite normal epicardial arteries.

Symptoms (classic triad):

  1. Angina (onset → average survival 5 years without treatment)
  2. Syncope (onset → average survival 3 years)
  3. Heart failure (onset → average survival 2 years)

Physical examination:

  • Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border, radiating to carotids
  • Murmur peaks later in systole as severity increases
  • Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, slow-rising carotid pulse)
  • Soft or absent A2
  • S4 (from stiff, hypertrophied LV)
  • Sustained, non-displaced apex beat
  • In severe AS with low output: Murmur may be deceptively soft

Severity grading (echocardiography):

ParameterMildModerateSevere
Aortic valve area (cm²)>1.51.0-1.5<1.0
Mean gradient (mmHg)<2525-40>40
Peak velocity (m/s)<3.03.0-4.0>4.0

Low-flow, low-gradient AS: Severe AS with reduced EF and low transvalvular gradient (<40 mmHg) due to poor LV function. Dobutamine stress echo helps differentiate true severe AS from pseudo-severe AS.

Treatment:

  • No effective medical therapy to slow progression
  • Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR): Standard for symptomatic severe AS in low/intermediate surgical risk patients
  • Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR): Approved for all surgical risk categories. Current devices: Edwards SAPIEN (balloon-expandable), CoreValve/Evolut (self-expanding), Lotus (mechanically expandable). Access: Usually transfemoral; alternatives include transapical, subclavian, transaortic. Success rate >90%; 30-day mortality 2-15% depending on risk profile. PARTNER trials demonstrated non-inferiority or superiority to SAVR across risk categories.

topics.title: Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
topics.content:
Etiology:

  • Valve leaflet disease: Bicuspid aortic valve, infective endocarditis, rheumatic, degenerative
  • Aortic root disease: Hypertension, Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, aortic dissection, syphilitic aortitis, ankylosing spondylitis

Pathophysiology:

  • Chronic AR: Volume overload → LV dilatation (eccentric hypertrophy) → Eventually systolic dysfunction
  • Acute AR (endocarditis, aortic dissection): Sudden volume overload on unprepared LV → Acute heart failure, pulmonary edema — surgical emergency

Clinical features of chronic severe AR:

  • Wide pulse pressure, low diastolic BP
  • Peripheral signs: Bounding pulses (Corrigan/water-hammer pulse), de Musset sign (head bobbing), Quincke sign (capillary pulsations in nail beds), Duroziez sign (to-and-fro femoral murmur), Hill sign (popliteal SBP > brachial SBP by >40 mmHg), pistol shot femorals
  • Murmur: High-pitched, blowing, early diastolic decrescendo murmur at left sternal border (or right sternal border if aortic root dilatation)
  • Austin Flint murmur: Low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex (functional mitral stenosis from AR jet impinging on anterior mitral leaflet)
  • Displaced, hyperdynamic apex beat

Treatment:

  • Vasodilator therapy (nifedipine, ACE inhibitors/ARBs) if hypertensive
  • Surgical indications: Symptomatic severe AR, or asymptomatic with LVEF <55% or LV end-systolic diameter >50 mm or indexed end-systolic dimension >25 mm/m²

topics.title: Mitral Stenosis (MS)
topics.content:
Etiology: Rheumatic heart disease (overwhelmingly the most common cause). Other rare causes: severe mitral annular calcification, congenital MS, SLE, carcinoid, radiation.

Pathophysiology: Obstruction to LV inflow → Elevated LA pressure → Pulmonary congestion → Pulmonary hypertension → Right heart failure. Normal mitral valve area: 4-6 cm².

Symptoms: Dyspnea on exertion (most common), orthopnea, PND, hemoptysis (from bronchial vein rupture), atrial fibrillation (due to LA dilatation), embolic events, Ortner syndrome (hoarseness from LA compression of recurrent laryngeal nerve).

Physical examination:

  • Loud S1 (if valve is mobile and pliable)
  • Opening snap (OS) after S2 — the shorter the S2-OS interval, the more severe the stenosis
  • Low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex (best heard in left lateral decubitus with bell of stethoscope)
  • Presystolic accentuation (if in sinus rhythm — due to atrial contraction)
  • Signs of pulmonary hypertension: Loud P2, RV heave, TR murmur
  • Malar flush (mitral facies): Pinkish-purple patches on cheeks due to low cardiac output and systemic vasoconstriction

Severity (echocardiography):

ParameterMildModerateSevere
Valve area (cm²)>1.51.0-1.5<1.0
Mean gradient (mmHg)<55-10>10

Treatment:

  • Rate control for AF (beta blockers, digoxin); anticoagulation
  • Percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy (PMBC): Preferred intervention for symptomatic moderate-to-severe MS with favorable valve anatomy (assessed by Wilkins echocardiographic score ≤8 — evaluates leaflet mobility, thickness, calcification, subvalvular thickening; each scored 0-4; total score ≤8 predicts good outcome). Contraindicated if significant MR or LA thrombus.
  • Mitral valve replacement (MVR): If unfavorable anatomy for PMBC or failed PMBC. Mechanical valves require lifelong anticoagulation.

topics.title: Mitral Regurgitation (MR)
topics.content:
Primary (organic) MR: Abnormality of the valve leaflets, chordae, or papillary muscles

  • Mitral valve prolapse (most common cause of primary MR in developed countries)
  • Rheumatic disease
  • Infective endocarditis
  • Radiation, connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos)

Secondary (functional) MR: Normal valve with annular dilatation and/or papillary muscle displacement due to LV dilatation/dysfunction (ischemic or nonischemic cardiomyopathy)

Pathophysiology:

  • Chronic MR: Volume overload → Eccentric LV hypertrophy and LA dilatation → Eventually LV systolic dysfunction
  • EF overestimates true LV function in MR (part of stroke volume goes into low-pressure LA)
  • Acute severe MR (chordal rupture, papillary muscle rupture post-MI, endocarditis): Sudden volume overload → Acute pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock

Physical examination:

  • Blowing, high-pitched, pansystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla
  • Direction of radiation may indicate etiology: Posterior leaflet prolapse → murmur radiates anteriorly to sternum; anterior leaflet prolapse → radiates posteriorly to back
  • Displaced, hyperdynamic apex beat
  • S3 (volume overload)

Treatment:

  • Primary MR: Surgical repair (preferred over replacement) when symptomatic or when LVEF ≤60% or LVESD ≥40 mm. Valve repair has better long-term outcomes than replacement.
  • Secondary MR: Optimize medical therapy for heart failure (GDMT); surgery controversial; transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER) with MitraClip shown to reduce hospitalizations and mortality in selected HF patients with functional MR (COAPT trial criteria)

topics.title: Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP)
topics.content:
Most common valvular abnormality (~2-3% of population). Myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve with displacement of one or both leaflets ≥2 mm above the mitral annulus into the LA during systole.

Auscultation: Mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur

  • Maneuvers that decrease LV volume (standing, Valsalva) → Click moves earlier, murmur becomes longer and louder
  • Maneuvers that increase LV volume (squatting) → Click moves later, murmur becomes shorter and softer

Complications (uncommon): Severe MR, infective endocarditis, arrhythmias, TIA/stroke, sudden cardiac death (rare, associated with bileaflet prolapse and significant MR)

topics.title: Tricuspid Valve Disease
topics.content:
Tricuspid regurgitation (TR):

  • Secondary TR (functional) from RV dilatation: By far the most common etiology (pulmonary hypertension, left-sided heart disease, RV infarction)
  • Primary TR: Endocarditis (IV drug use), rheumatic, Ebstein anomaly, carcinoid syndrome, radiation, pacemaker/ICD leads
  • Carvallo sign: TR murmur increases with inspiration
  • Clinical features: Elevated JVP with prominent v wave, pulsatile liver (hepatomegaly), peripheral edema, ascites

Tricuspid stenosis: Rare; almost always rheumatic (always coexists with mitral valve disease). Diastolic murmur at left lower sternal border, augmented by inspiration.

topics.title: Pulmonic Valve Disease
topics.content:
Pulmonic stenosis: Usually congenital; systolic murmur at left upper sternal border; increases with inspiration. Treatment: Balloon valvuloplasty for significant stenosis.

Pulmonic regurgitation: Most commonly functional (due to pulmonary hypertension) — Graham Steell murmur (high-pitched, early diastolic). Primary PR: post-valvotomy, endocarditis, carcinoid.

topics.title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease / Stable Angina
topics.content:
Pathophysiology: Imbalance between myocardial O2 supply and demand. Supply depends on: coronary blood flow (diastolic pressure, coronary artery patency, coronary vasomotor tone) and O2 content. Demand depends on: heart rate, contractility, and wall stress (preload and afterload).

Clinical presentation: Retrosternal chest pressure/heaviness precipitated by exertion or emotional stress, lasting 2-10 minutes, relieved by rest or nitroglycerin within 5 minutes. May radiate to left arm, jaw, neck, or back. May present as "anginal equivalents": dyspnea, fatigue, diaphoresis (especially in elderly, women, and diabetic patients).

Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS) Classification:

  • Class I: Ordinary physical activity does not cause angina; angina with strenuous activity
  • Class II: Slight limitation; angina with walking >2 blocks or climbing >1 flight of stairs
  • Class III: Marked limitation; angina with walking 1-2 blocks or climbing 1 flight
  • Class IV: Inability to carry out any activity without angina; angina at rest

Investigations:

  • Resting ECG: May be normal; may show ST-T changes, Q waves, LBBB, LVH
  • Exercise stress test: ST depression ≥1 mm (horizontal or downsloping) is positive
  • Stress imaging if unable to exercise or uninterpretable ECG (see Part 3)
  • Coronary angiography: Gold standard for defining coronary anatomy

Medical Therapy for Stable Angina:

Anti-ischemic drugs:

  • Beta blockers (first-line): Reduce heart rate, contractility, BP → Decrease myocardial O2 demand. Metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol.
  • Nitrates: Venodilation (decrease preload) and coronary vasodilation. Sublingual NTG for acute episodes; long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mononitrate) for prophylaxis. Requires a nitrate-free interval (~10-12 hours) to prevent tolerance.
  • Calcium channel blockers: Dihydropyridines (amlodipine, nifedipine — arterial vasodilation, may cause reflex tachycardia), non-dihydropyridines (verapamil, diltiazem — also reduce heart rate and contractility; avoid in HFrEF and do not combine with beta blockers that risk excessive bradycardia)
  • Ranolazine: Inhibits late Na+ current, reducing intracellular Ca2+ overload. Anti-ischemic without hemodynamic effects. Used as add-on therapy. Contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors. Prolongs QT.

Disease-modifying/prognostic therapy:

  • Aspirin (75-100 mg/day): All patients unless contraindicated
  • Statins: High-intensity (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg); target LDL <70 mg/dL (or <55 mg/dL in very high risk)
  • ACE inhibitors/ARBs: Especially if hypertension, diabetes, LV dysfunction, or CKD
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Emerging role in cardiovascular risk reduction

Revascularization (PCI vs CABG):

  • ISCHEMIA trial: In stable patients with moderate-to-severe ischemia, initial invasive strategy did not reduce death, MI, or hospitalization for heart failure compared to optimal medical therapy
  • PCI preferred: 1-2 vessel disease, no diabetes, favorable anatomy
  • CABG preferred: Left main disease, 3-vessel disease (especially with diabetes — FREEDOM trial), reduced LVEF, complex anatomy (high SYNTAX score)
  • PCI: Drug-eluting stents (DES) with DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor — clopidogrel/ticagrelor/prasugrel) for typically 6-12 months

topics.title: Non-ST-Elevation ACS (NSTE-ACS)
topics.content:
Encompasses both NSTEMI (elevated troponin) and unstable angina (no troponin elevation). Pathophysiology: Non-occlusive thrombus on a disrupted atherosclerotic plaque (fissure or erosion).

TIMI Risk Score for NSTE-ACS (each factor = 1 point, total 0-7):

  1. Age ≥65 years
  2. ≥3 CAD risk factors
  3. Known CAD (≥50% stenosis)
  4. Aspirin use in prior 7 days
  5. ≥2 anginal events in prior 24 hours
  6. ST deviation ≥0.5 mm
  7. Elevated cardiac biomarkers

Higher score → Higher 14-day risk of death, MI, or urgent revascularization

HEART Score (used in ED evaluation): History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin (each scored 0-2)

Treatment:

Anti-ischemic therapy:

  • Beta blockers (avoid in acute HF, cardiogenic shock, bradycardia, reactive airway disease)
  • Nitroglycerin (sublingual → IV if ongoing ischemia; avoid if SBP <90, recent PDE5 inhibitor use, or RV infarction)
  • Morphine (for refractory pain; use cautiously)

Antithrombotic therapy:

  • Aspirin: 162-325 mg loading, then 81 mg daily
  • P2Y12 inhibitors: Clopidogrel (300-600 mg load), ticagrelor (180 mg load — preferred in most), or prasugrel (60 mg load, 10 mg maintenance; contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA; caution/reduced dose 5 mg in age ≥75 or weight <60 kg)
  • Anticoagulation: Unfractionated heparin (UFH), enoxaparin (LMWH), fondaparinux, or bivalirudin during acute phase

Invasive vs Conservative strategy:

  • Early invasive strategy (angiography within 24-72 hours): Recommended for high-risk patients (TIMI ≥3, elevated troponin, ST changes, recurrent ischemia, hemodynamic instability, heart failure, diabetes, prior CABG/PCI, high GRACE score)
  • Conservative strategy: Low-risk patients without high-risk features

Prinzmetal (Variant) Angina: Coronary vasospasm causing transient ST elevation. Occurs at rest, often in early morning hours. Coronary angiography may be normal or show nonobstructive disease. Treatment: Calcium channel blockers and nitrates. Beta blockers may be harmful (unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction). Avoid smoking and cocaine.

topics.title: ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
topics.content:
Pathophysiology: Complete thrombotic occlusion of an epicardial coronary artery, usually due to rupture of a vulnerable atherosclerotic plaque. Necrosis begins in the subendocardium and extends transmurally over 4-12 hours ("wavefront of necrosis").

Universal Definition of MI — 5 Types:

  1. Type 1: Spontaneous MI due to atherosclerotic plaque disruption
  2. Type 2: MI secondary to supply-demand mismatch (tachyarrhythmia, hypotension, anemia, respiratory failure, coronary spasm)
  3. Type 3: MI resulting in death before biomarkers obtained
  4. Type 4a: MI related to PCI; 4b: MI related to stent thrombosis
  5. Type 5: MI related to CABG

Clinical presentation:

  • Severe, crushing, retrosternal chest pain lasting >20 minutes, not relieved by rest/NTG
  • Associated symptoms: Diaphoresis, nausea/vomiting, dyspnea, lightheadedness
  • Physical findings vary: May be normal; S4 gallop, new murmur (MR from papillary muscle dysfunction), signs of HF (S3, rales, elevated JVP)

Laboratory findings:

  • Cardiac troponin (cTnT/cTnI): Most sensitive and specific biomarker. Begins rising 3-4 hours after symptom onset; peaks at 18-36 hours; remains elevated for 10-14 days.
  • CK-MB: Rises 4-8 hours, peaks 24 hours, normalizes 48-72 hours. Useful for detecting reinfarction due to shorter elevation window.

ECG criteria for STEMI:

  • New ST elevation ≥1 mm in two contiguous limb leads, or ≥2 mm in two contiguous precordial leads
  • New LBBB with clinical suspicion of acute MI (Sgarbossa criteria)

Reperfusion Therapy — The cornerstone of STEMI management:

Primary PCI (preferred):

  • Target: Door-to-balloon time ≤90 minutes (≤120 minutes if transfer to PCI-capable center)
  • Superior to fibrinolysis when available in timely fashion
  • Can be performed regardless of time from symptom onset (within 12 hours ideally)
  • TIMI flow grade 3 (normal flow) is the goal; achieved in >90% with modern techniques

Fibrinolysis:

  • If PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes
  • Most effective within 12 hours of symptom onset; greatest benefit within 3 hours
  • Target: Door-to-needle time ≤30 minutes
  • Agents: Tenecteplase (TNK — weight-based single bolus, most practical), alteplase (tPA — accelerated regimen), reteplase, streptokinase
  • Absolute contraindications: Active internal bleeding (excluding menses), history of hemorrhagic stroke, ischemic stroke within 3 months, intracranial neoplasm/AVM, known structural cerebrovascular lesion, suspected aortic dissection, significant head/facial trauma within 3 months
  • If fibrinolysis fails (persistent ST elevation and ongoing symptoms 60-90 min after administration): Rescue PCI

Adjunctive STEMI Therapy:

  • Aspirin: 162-325 mg chewed immediately; then 81 mg daily indefinitely
  • P2Y12 inhibitor: Ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel with primary PCI
  • Anticoagulation: Heparin (UFH) during PCI
  • Beta blocker: Oral within 24 hours (if no HF, cardiogenic shock, or high-grade AV block; avoid IV beta blockers in acute setting per COMMIT trial)
  • ACE inhibitor/ARB: Within 24 hours (especially if anterior MI, HF, LVEF ≤40%)
  • High-intensity statin: Start immediately
  • Nitroglycerin: For ongoing ischemia or hypertension (avoid if RV infarction, SBP <90, recent PDE5 inhibitor)
  • Aldosterone antagonist: If LVEF ≤40% + HF symptoms or diabetes (eplerenone — EPHESUS trial)

Complications of STEMI:

  • Arrhythmias: VF (most common cause of early death), VT, accelerated idioventricular rhythm (reperfusion arrhythmia, benign), complete heart block (especially inferior MI), AF
  • Mechanical: Free wall rupture (tamponade, PEA), ventricular septal rupture (new holosystolic murmur, step-up in O2 saturation from RA to RV), papillary muscle rupture (acute severe MR — more common in inferoposterior MI)
  • LV aneurysm: Persistent ST elevation weeks after MI, mural thrombus formation
  • Pericarditis: Early (within days — fibrinous, diffuse ST elevation, pleuritic pain; treat with aspirin, avoid anticoagulation if large effusion), late Dressler syndrome (2-10 weeks post-MI — autoimmune, pericardial effusion)
  • RV infarction: Complicates 30-50% of inferior MIs. Triad: Hypotension, elevated JVP, clear lungs. ST elevation in V4R. Treatment: Volume loading (IV fluids), avoid nitrates and diuretics; consider inotropes.
  • Cardiogenic shock: Mortality ~50%; revascularization is the most important intervention; IABP (IABP-SHOCK II trial showed no mortality benefit); Impella or VA-ECMO for refractory cases

topics.title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
topics.content:
Drug-eluting stents (DES): Second-generation DES (everolimus-eluting, zotarolimus-eluting) are standard. Restenosis rates: 5-15% with DES (vs 20-30% with bare metal stents).

DAPT duration:

  • Post-ACS: Typically 12 months
  • Elective PCI: 6 months (may be shortened to 3 months or extended to >12 months based on ischemic vs bleeding risk balance)
  • Early DAPT discontinuation (within first month) markedly increases stent thrombosis risk

Stent thrombosis: Acute (<24h), subacute (1-30 days), late (30 days-1 year), very late (>1 year). Presents as STEMI or sudden death. Mortality 10-20%. Prevention: DAPT adherence, adequate stent expansion.

Restenosis: Neointimal hyperplasia causing re-narrowing. Usually presents as recurrent angina within 12 months. Treatment: Repeat PCI with drug-eluting stent or balloon.

topics.title: Acute Pericarditis
topics.content:
Etiology: Idiopathic/viral (most common in developed countries), tuberculosis (most common in endemic areas), post-MI (early and Dressler syndrome), uremia, malignancy, autoimmune (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis), radiation, post-cardiac surgery (postpericardiotomy syndrome), drugs (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid).

Clinical features:

  • Sharp, pleuritic chest pain improved by sitting up and leaning forward, worse with inspiration and lying supine
  • Pericardial friction rub: Scratchy, high-pitched, best heard at left lower sternal border with patient leaning forward; has up to 3 components (atrial systole, ventricular systole, early diastolic filling)
  • ECG: Diffuse concave-upward ST elevation with PR depression (ST elevation in all leads except aVR; PR depression in all leads except aVR — in aVR: ST depression and PR elevation)
  • Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP)

Treatment:

  • NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg TID or aspirin 750-1000 mg TID) for 1-2 weeks with gradual taper
  • Colchicine (0.5 mg once daily for patients ≤70 kg; 0.5 mg BID for patients >70 kg): Added to NSAIDs; reduces recurrence by ~50% (COPE, CORE trials). Continue for 3 months.
  • Corticosteroids: Reserved for refractory cases or contraindication to NSAIDs/colchicine; associated with higher recurrence rate; use low dose with slow taper
  • Avoid anticoagulation in acute pericarditis (risk of hemorrhagic pericarditis)

topics.title: Cardiac Tamponade
topics.content:
Accumulation of pericardial fluid under pressure compressing the heart and compromising filling.

Beck's triad (acute tamponade): Hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds

Clinical features:

  • Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration) — most reliable clinical sign
  • Tachycardia
  • Elevated JVP with prominent x descent but absent y descent
  • Electrical alternans on ECG (alternating QRS amplitude)
  • Low-voltage QRS

Echocardiographic findings:

  • Pericardial effusion
  • RA diastolic collapse (most sensitive early sign)
  • RV diastolic collapse (more specific)
  • Respiratory variation in mitral and tricuspid inflow velocities (>25% mitral, >40% tricuspid)
  • Dilated IVC without respiratory collapse

Treatment: Pericardiocentesis (urgent/emergent). IV fluids as temporary bridge.

topics.title: Constrictive Pericarditis
topics.content:
Chronic thickening and calcification of the pericardium restricting diastolic filling.

Causes: Idiopathic, post-radiation, post-cardiac surgery, tuberculosis, chronic pericarditis of any etiology.

Clinical features mimic right heart failure: Elevated JVP, peripheral edema, ascites (often out of proportion to peripheral edema), hepatomegaly.

Key distinguishing features:
FeatureConstrictive PericarditisRestrictive Cardiomyopathy
PericardiumThickened/calcifiedNormal
Kussmaul signPresentMay be present
Pericardial knockPresent (early S3-like sound)Absent
Septal bouncePresentAbsent
Respiratory variation in flowsEnhanced (ventricular interdependence)Absent
CT/CMR pericardium>4 mm thicknessNormal
BNPNormal or mildly elevatedMarkedly elevated

Treatment: Pericardiectomy (surgical stripping of pericardium) is definitive. Medical therapy has limited role.

topics.title: Cardiac Tumors
topics.content:
• Myxoma: Most common primary cardiac tumor. ~75% in left atrium, attached to interatrial septum near fossa ovalis. Can cause emboli, obstruction (mimicking mitral stenosis — "tumor plop"), and constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, elevated ESR). "Wrecking ball" effect. Treatment: Surgical excision.
  • Papillary fibroelastoma: Second most common benign tumor; often on valve surfaces; risk of emboli.
  • Rhabdomyoma: Most common cardiac tumor in children; associated with tuberous sclerosis; may regress spontaneously.
  • Malignant primary tumors: Sarcomas (angiosarcoma most common — predilection for right atrium; poor prognosis).
  • Metastatic tumors: ~20-fold more common than primary cardiac tumors. Most common primaries: Lung, breast, melanoma, lymphoma, leukemia.

topics.title: Epidemiology and Definition
topics.content:
Hypertension affects >1 billion people worldwide and is one of the leading causes of global morbidity and mortality. It doubles the risk of CHD, CHF, ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke, renal failure, and PAD.

2017 ACC/AHA Blood Pressure Classification:

CategorySystolic (mmHg)Diastolic (mmHg)
Normal<120and<80
Elevated120-129and<80
Hypertension Stage 1130-139or80-89
Hypertension Stage 2≥140or>90

Blood pressure should be measured in both arms at the first visit. An average of 2-3 readings on 2-3 separate occasions should be obtained. Out-of-office measurements (ambulatory or home BP monitoring) are helpful for confirming the diagnosis.

White coat hypertension (15-20% of patients): Elevated office BP but normal out-of-office BP. Long-term outcomes similar to normotensive individuals.

Masked hypertension (10-30%): Normal office BP but elevated out-of-office BP. Carries cardiovascular risk similar to sustained hypertension.

topics.title: Mechanisms of Hypertension
topics.content:
Arterial pressure = Cardiac output × Peripheral resistance

Key regulatory systems:
  1. Renal sodium handling (intravascular volume): Kidney is both a target and cause of hypertension. NaCl retention → volume expansion → increased CO → increased BP. The "pressure-natriuresis" phenomenon requires higher pressures to excrete sodium in hypertensives.
  2. Autonomic nervous system: Sympathetic overactivity increases heart rate, contractility, vasoconstriction, and renal sodium reabsorption. α1-receptors: Vasoconstriction. β1-receptors: Increased HR and contractility. β2-receptors: Vasodilation.
  3. Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS):
    • Renin (from juxtaglomerular cells) → Converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin I
    • ACE (in pulmonary endothelium) → Converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II
    • Angiotensin II → AT1 receptor: Vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, sodium retention, sympathetic activation, cardiac/vascular remodeling
    • AT2 receptor: Vasodilation, sodium excretion, inhibits growth
    • Aldosterone → Sodium retention via ENaC in collecting duct; also promotes myocardial fibrosis, vascular inflammation
  4. Vascular mechanisms: Vascular resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius (small changes in lumen diameter have large effects). Vascular remodeling (hypertrophic or eutrophic) increases resistance. Endothelial dysfunction reduces NO bioavailability.

topics.title: Pathologic Consequences of Hypertension
topics.content:
Heart: LV hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, heart failure (both HFpEF and HFrEF), coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, sudden death.

Brain: Ischemic stroke (85%), hemorrhagic stroke, TIA, vascular dementia, hypertensive encephalopathy (autoregulation failure at MAP >150-200 mmHg — headache, nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances, seizures, altered mental status).

Kidney: Nephrosclerosis, proteinuria, progressive CKD, ESRD. Renal risk more closely related to systolic than diastolic BP. Black men at greatest risk.

Peripheral arteries: Atherosclerotic PAD, aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection.

Eyes: Hypertensive retinopathy (arteriolar narrowing, AV nicking, hemorrhages, exudates, papilledema in malignant hypertension).

topics.title: Primary (Essential) Hypertension
topics.content:
Accounts for ~80-95% of hypertension. Multifactorial — genetic susceptibility interacting with environmental factors (high sodium intake, obesity, physical inactivity, excessive alcohol, psychosocial stress).

Plasma renin activity (PRA) profiling: ~10-15% have high PRA (vasoconstrictor phenotype), ~25% have low PRA (volume-dependent phenotype).

African Americans: Higher prevalence, more severe, earlier onset; tend to have low-renin, volume-dependent hypertension; respond better to diuretics and CCBs; may require higher doses of ACEi/ARBs.

topics.title: Secondary Hypertension
topics.content:
Accounts for 5-20% of cases. Should be suspected with: onset <30 or >55 years, severe or resistant hypertension, sudden onset, clinical clues to secondary cause.

Causes and clues:

Renal parenchymal disease (most common secondary cause): >80% of CKD patients have hypertension. Evaluate with urinalysis, serum creatinine, renal ultrasound.

Renovascular hypertension: Atherosclerotic (older patients, generalized atherosclerosis) or fibromuscular dysplasia (young women, "string of beads" on angiography). Clues: Abdominal bruit (extends into diastole), flash pulmonary edema, renal function deterioration with ACEi/ARB, refractory hypertension. Imaging: Doppler ultrasound, MRA, CTA. Treatment: Medical therapy generally first-line; renal artery stenting may be considered for fibromuscular dysplasia.

Primary aldosteronism: Prevalence 5-15% of hypertensives. Causes: Aldosterone-producing adenoma (~35%), bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (~60%). Features: Hypertension, hypokalemia (may be absent in many), metabolic alkalosis. Screening: PA/PRA ratio >30:1 with PA >555 pmol/L (>20 ng/dL). Confirmatory: Failure to suppress aldosterone with salt loading, fludrocortisone, or captopril. Lateralization: Adrenal CT → Adrenal venous sampling. Treatment: Unilateral adenoma → Laparoscopic adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia → Aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone/eplerenone).

Pheochromocytoma: Catecholamine-secreting tumor from adrenal medulla (~0.05% of hypertensives). ~20% are familial (MEN 2A/2B, von Hippel-Lindau, NF1, paraganglioma syndromes with SDH mutations). Classic triad: Episodic headache, sweating, palpitations with paroxysmal hypertension. Diagnosis: 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines or plasma-free metanephrines. Imaging: CT or MRI of abdomen and pelvis. Treatment: Surgical excision; preoperative alpha blockade (phenoxybenzamine) first, then beta blockade.

Cushing syndrome: Cortisol excess → hypertension in 75-80%. Screening: 24-hour urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test, late-night salivary cortisol.

Obstructive sleep apnea: Present in >50% of hypertensive patients with OSA. Severity of hypertension correlates with severity of sleep apnea. CPAP/BiPAP may modestly improve BP.

Coarctation of the aorta: Most common congenital cardiovascular cause of hypertension. Upper extremity hypertension with diminished femoral pulses and radio-femoral delay; systolic pressure gradient between arms and legs. Associated with bicuspid aortic valve, Turner syndrome.

Thyroid disease: Hypothyroidism → mild diastolic hypertension; Hyperthyroidism → systolic hypertension.

Rare Mendelian forms: Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (GRA), Liddle syndrome (gain-of-function ENaC mutation), 11β-hydroxylase deficiency, 17α-hydroxylase deficiency, apparent mineralocorticoid excess, Gordon syndrome.

topics.title: Treatment of Hypertension
topics.content:
Lifestyle modifications:

  • Weight reduction (maintain BMI <25 kg/m²): ~5-6 mmHg SBP reduction per 10 kg lost
  • Dietary salt reduction (<5-6 g NaCl/day or <2.3 g Na/day): 3.7-5 mmHg reduction
  • DASH diet (rich in fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, reduced saturated fat)
  • Moderate alcohol consumption (≤2 drinks/day men, ≤1 drink/day women)
  • Regular aerobic exercise (30 min/day most days): 4-9 mmHg reduction

When to initiate pharmacologic therapy (2017 ACC/AHA):

  • Known CVD or 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%: Start drugs at BP ≥130/80
  • No CVD and 10-year ASCVD risk <10%: Start drugs at BP ≥140/90

Blood pressure goals:

  • For most adults with hypertension + known CVD or 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%: Target <130/80 mmHg
  • Exceptions with more conservative targets (<130/80 possible, but <140/90 more practical): Patients >80 years, those at risk of falls, those with advanced CKD

Major drug classes:

Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone): Inhibit Na+-Cl- cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule. Side effects: Hypokalemia, hyperuricemia (gout), hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, hyponatremia. Chlorthalidone has longer half-life and greater antihypertensive potency than HCTZ.

ACE inhibitors (captopril, lisinopril, ramipril): Block conversion of angiotensin I to II. Also increase bradykinin (may cause cough in ~15% and angioedema in <1%). Contraindicated in pregnancy and bilateral renal artery stenosis. Compelling indications: Post-MI, HFrEF, diabetic nephropathy, CKD with proteinuria.

Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) (losartan, valsartan, candesartan): Selective AT1 blockade. Similar efficacy to ACEi with less cough and angioedema. Same contraindications. Do NOT combine ACEi + ARB (increased adverse events without benefit per ONTARGET trial).

Calcium channel blockers:

  • Dihydropyridines (amlodipine, nifedipine): Arterial vasodilation; reflex tachycardia. Side effects: Peripheral edema, flushing, headache.
  • Non-dihydropyridines (verapamil, diltiazem): Also reduce heart rate. Contraindicated: HFrEF, second/third-degree heart block, sick sinus syndrome.

Beta blockers (metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol, carvedilol, labetalol): Decrease heart rate, contractility, renin release. Compelling indications: Post-MI, HFrEF, rate control in AF, angina. Contraindications: Severe bradycardia, decompensated HF, asthma (cardioselective agents may be used cautiously in COPD).

Other agents:

  • Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone): Particularly effective for resistant hypertension, primary aldosteronism, HFrEF
  • Alpha blockers (prazosin, doxazosin, terazosin): Second/third-line; useful in prostatism; doxazosin inferior to chlorthalidone for heart failure prevention (ALLHAT)
  • Direct renin inhibitor (aliskiren): Not first-line; useful in diabetic nephropathy
  • Central sympatholytics (clonidine, methyldopa): Rebound hypertension with abrupt withdrawal of clonidine; methyldopa preferred in pregnancy
  • Direct vasodilators (hydralazine, minoxidil): Reserved for refractory cases; hydralazine may cause lupus-like syndrome; minoxidil causes fluid retention and hirsutism

Resistant hypertension: BP persistently >140/90 despite three antihypertensive agents at optimal doses (including a diuretic). Prevalence ~12-15% of treated hypertensives. Workup: Confirm true resistance (exclude white coat, nonadherence, pseudoresistance), evaluate secondary causes, add spironolactone (most effective add-on per PATHWAY-2 trial).

topics.title: Hypertensive Emergencies and Urgencies
topics.content:
Hypertensive urgency: Severe hypertension (SBP ≥180 or DBP ≥120) without acute target organ damage. Treatment: Gradual oral BP reduction over 24-48 hours.

Hypertensive emergency: Severe hypertension WITH acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, acute stroke, aortic dissection, acute MI, acute HF/pulmonary edema, eclampsia, acute renal failure, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia). Requires IV antihypertensive therapy in an ICU setting.

Target: Reduce MAP by no more than 25% within minutes to 2 hours, then to 160/100-110 over 2-6 hours (except in aortic dissection — target SBP ≤120 and HR <60 rapidly).

IV agents for hypertensive emergencies:

AgentIndication
NitroprussideHypertensive encephalopathy (risk of cyanide toxicity with prolonged use)
NicardipineStroke, most emergencies
LabetalolMost emergencies, aortic dissection, stroke
EsmololAortic dissection, perioperative
NitroglycerinAcute MI, acute HF
ClevidipinePerioperative hypertension
EnalaprilatAcute LV failure
PhentolamineAdrenergic crisis (pheochromocytoma)
HydralazinePreeclampsia/eclampsia

Malignant hypertension: Abrupt BP rise with diffuse necrotizing vasculitis, arteriolar thrombi, fibrinoid necrosis in arterioles. Features: Progressive retinopathy (hemorrhages, exudates, papilledema), deteriorating renal function, proteinuria, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, encephalopathy. Requires urgent IV antihypertensive therapy.

topics.title: Diseases of the Aorta
topics.content:
Thoracic Aortic Aneurysms
