Medial degeneration is the most common pathology of ascending aortic aneurysms. Average growth: 0.1-0.2 cm/year. Risk of rupture: 2-3%/year for <4 cm; up to 7%/year for >6 cm.

Associated conditions: Marfan syndrome (fibrillin-1), Loeys-Dietz syndrome (TGFBR1/2), Ehlers-Danlos type IV, bicuspid aortic valve, Turner syndrome, familial thoracic aortic aneurysm syndromes.

Treatment: Beta blockers to reduce rate of expansion (especially Marfan). ARBs (losartan) may reduce rate of aortic root enlargement in Marfan by blocking TGF-β signaling. Surgical repair: Ascending aorta ≥5.5 cm (≥4.5-5.0 cm in Marfan or bicuspid AV); descending aorta ≥6.0 cm.

Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms (AAA)

Most (90%) are related to atherosclerosis. Screening: One-time ultrasound for men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked. Rupture risk: <5 cm: 1-2%/year; >5 cm: 20-40% over 5 years. Repair: Elective at ≥5.5 cm or growth >0.5 cm/6 months. EVAR (endovascular) has lower short-term morbidity; comparable long-term mortality to open repair.

Aortic Dissection

Classification:

  • Stanford Type A: Involves ascending aorta (regardless of origin) → Surgical emergency
  • Stanford Type B: Descending aorta only (distal to left subclavian)
  • DeBakey: Type I (ascending + descending), Type II (ascending only), Type III (descending only)

Risk factors: Hypertension (most common, 70%), Marfan/Loeys-Dietz, bicuspid aortic valve, aortic coarctation, Turner syndrome, inflammatory aortitis, cocaine use, pregnancy (3rd trimester), weight lifting.

Clinical features: Sudden-onset severe tearing chest/back pain migrating with propagation. Unequal BP/pulses. Aortic regurgitation murmur (Type A). Neurologic deficits if carotid involvement. Tamponade if rupture into pericardium.

Diagnosis: CT angiography (sensitivity/specificity >95%), TEE (especially if hemodynamically unstable), MRI (>95% but time-consuming).

Treatment:

  • Type A: Emergency surgery (graft replacement of ascending aorta ± valve repair)
  • Type B (uncomplicated): Medical therapy — IV beta blocker (esmolol, labetalol) to target HR <60 and SBP ≤120 mmHg; add nitroprusside if needed after beta blockade
  • Type B (complicated — malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain): Endovascular stent-graft (TEVAR) or surgery

topics.title: Peripheral Artery Disease
topics.content:
Atherosclerotic PAD primarily affects the lower extremities. Risk factors are the same as for coronary atherosclerosis; smoking and diabetes are particularly strong risk factors.

Clinical features: Intermittent claudication (most common symptom — exertional leg pain relieved by rest); critical limb ischemia (rest pain, ulcers, gangrene). Physical findings: Diminished/absent pulses, bruits, skin changes, hair loss, cool extremities.

Ankle-brachial index (ABI): Normal 1.00-1.40; borderline 0.91-0.99; diagnostic of PAD <0.90; >1.40 suggests noncompressible arteries (calcification).

Prognosis: ~1/3 to 1/2 have coexisting CAD. 5-year mortality 15-25%. Death most commonly from cardiovascular causes.

Treatment: Risk factor modification (smoking cessation is critical), antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel), statins, ACE inhibitors. Exercise training (supervised walking program 30-45 min, 3×/week for 12 weeks). Cilostazol (PDE3 inhibitor — increases claudication distance by 40-60%). Revascularization for disabling claudication refractory to exercise/medical therapy, or critical limb ischemia.

topics.title: Other Vascular Conditions
topics.content:
Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease): Inflammatory occlusive disease of small and medium arteries and veins in distal extremities. Young male smokers. Segmental occlusions, no atherosclerosis proximally. Treatment: Smoking cessation (only effective measure).

Raynaud phenomenon: Episodic digital ischemia — blanching → cyanosis → rubor on cold exposure. Primary (idiopathic, benign, young women) vs secondary (scleroderma [80-90%], SLE, dermatomyositis, atherosclerosis, thoracic outlet syndrome, vibration injury, drugs). Treatment: Avoid cold, calcium channel blockers (nifedipine), PDE5 inhibitors for severe cases.

topics.title: Pulmonary Hypertension
topics.content:
Definition: Mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg on right heart catheterization. For precapillary PH (including PAH): mPAP >20 mmHg AND pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) ≥3 Wood units AND pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) ≤15 mmHg.

Updated WHO Classification (Groups):

  1. Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH): Idiopathic, heritable (BMPR2 mutations), drug-induced, connective tissue disease, HIV, portal hypertension, congenital heart disease
  2. PH due to left heart disease: Most common cause of PH (HFpEF, HFrEF, valvular disease)
  3. PH due to lung disease/hypoxia: COPD, ILD, sleep-disordered breathing, high altitude
  4. PH due to chronic thromboembolic disease (CTEPH): Potentially curable with pulmonary endarterectomy
  5. PH with unclear/multifactorial mechanisms: Sarcoidosis, hematologic disorders, metabolic disorders

PAH pathobiology: Obliterative arteriopathy with plexogenic remodeling of small pulmonary arterioles. Key molecular pathways: Endothelin, nitric oxide, prostacyclin.

Clinical features: Progressive dyspnea, fatigue, syncope (with exertion), chest pain, signs of right heart failure.

Diagnosis: Echocardiography (estimated RVSP, RV dilatation/dysfunction) → Right heart catheterization (confirms diagnosis and hemodynamics). Vasoreactivity testing with inhaled NO or IV prostacyclin.

Treatment of PAH:

  • Vasoreactive (~5% of PAH): High-dose calcium channel blockers (nifedipine or diltiazem)
  • Non-vasoreactive: Combination oral therapy with:
    • Endothelin receptor antagonists (bosentan, ambrisentan, macitentan)
    • PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) — increase cGMP → vasodilation
    • Soluble guanylyl cyclase stimulator (riociguat) — approved for PAH and CTEPH
    • Prostacyclin pathway agents (epoprostenol [IV], treprostinil [IV/SC/inhaled/oral], iloprost [inhaled], selexipag [oral IP receptor agonist])
  • AMBITION trial: Upfront combination therapy (ambrisentan + tadalafil) superior to monotherapy, with 50% lower risk of clinical worsening
  • Triple therapy escalation if deterioration on dual therapy
  • Lung transplantation for severe, refractory PAH

topics.title: Congenital Heart Disease — Common Lesions
topics.content:
Atrial Septal Defect (ASD):

  • Ostium secundum (most common, ~75%): Defect at fossa ovalis
  • Ostium primum: Part of AV canal defect; associated with mitral valve cleft
  • Sinus venosus: Near SVC-RA junction; associated with partial anomalous pulmonary venous return
  • Physical: Fixed split S2 (hallmark), systolic flow murmur across pulmonic valve (increased flow)
  • May present in adulthood with dyspnea, AF, paradoxical embolism, or RV failure
  • Closure: Percutaneous device closure for secundum ASD; surgical for primum and sinus venosus

Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD):

  • Most common congenital heart defect at birth; many close spontaneously
  • Membranous (most common in adults), muscular, inlet, outlet
  • Small restrictive VSD: Loud pansystolic murmur, minimal hemodynamic effect
  • Large nonrestrictive VSD: May lead to Eisenmenger syndrome

Coarctation of the Aorta:

  • Narrowing of the aorta, usually just distal to left subclavian artery
  • Upper extremity hypertension, diminished femoral pulses, radio-femoral delay
  • Rib notching on chest X-ray (from dilated intercostal collateral arteries)
  • Associated with bicuspid aortic valve, cerebral aneurysms (Berry aneurysms)

Tetralogy of Fallot (most common cyanotic CHD surviving to adulthood):

  • VSD, overriding aorta, RVOT obstruction (infundibular/valvular), RV hypertrophy
  • "Tet spells" in childhood: Cyanotic episodes treated with knee-chest position
  • Adults post-repair: Pulmonary regurgitation (most common long-term issue), RV dilatation, arrhythmias

Eisenmenger Syndrome:

  • Irreversible pulmonary vascular disease from long-standing left-to-right shunt → Shunt reversal (right-to-left) → Cyanosis
  • Once established, the underlying defect should NOT be closed
  • Features: Cyanosis, clubbing, polycythemia, hemoptysis, paradoxical emboli
  • PAH-targeted therapy may improve symptoms. Heart-lung transplant for severe cases.

Source: Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21st Edition. Chapters 236-283.

This study guide is intended as a revision aid and should be used alongside the original textbook for comprehensive understanding.
